stem: Based on the EHR, which actions must the nurse take before administering the newly ordered STAT medications? Select all that apply.

options.text: Place the client on continuous cardiac monitoring.
Administer the furosemide 40 mg IV push as ordered.
Notify the healthcare provider of the current vital signs and lab results.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed
tabs.content:
Admission Note - Yesterday, 16:00
S: Robert Jones is a 72-year-old male admitted from his primary care provider's office with a chief complaint of worsening shortness of breath, fatigue, and a 10 lb weight gain over the past week.
B: He has a history of congestive heart failure (EF 35%), hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. Client lives alone in a second-floor apartment with no elevator and reports difficulty affording fresh groceries, often relying on canned soups. He reports non-adherence to his low-sodium diet.
A: On admission, client was dyspneic with minimal exertion. Bilateral crackles noted in lower lung bases. 2+ pitting edema in lower extremities. Vitals: BP 148/88 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 22, SpO2 94% on room air.
R: Admitted to the medical unit for management of acute decompensated heart failure. Initiated on 2L O2 via nasal cannula.

Progress Note - Today, 08:00
S: Client reports feeling more short of breath this morning than yesterday. States, "I feel like I can't catch my breath, and my legs are more swollen."
B: Overnight, client had difficulty sleeping and required the head of the bed to be elevated to 90 degrees. Urine output has been minimal. No chest pain reported.
A: Current vitals: BP 100/60 mmHg (MAP 73), HR 58 bpm, RR 24, SpO2 92% on 2L NC. Auscultation reveals crackles halfway up the lung fields bilaterally. 3+ pitting edema noted to mid-shin. Client is alert and oriented x4. Morning labs show hypokalemia and elevated creatinine.
R: STAT orders for IV furosemide and IV digoxin received. Will hold digoxin due to bradycardia and notify provider of all findings before administering any new medications.

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Admission Assessment (Yesterday, 16:00)
General: Alert and oriented, appears fatigued.
Cardiovascular: S1/S2 audible, regular rhythm, no murmurs. JVD present at 30 degrees. Capillary refill <3 seconds.
Respiratory: Labored breathing on exertion. Bilateral crackles in lower lung bases.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Moves all extremities with equal strength.
Gastrointestinal: Abdomen soft, non-tender, bowel sounds active in all quadrants.
Extremities: 2+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities.

Focused Assessment (Today, 08:00)
Cardiovascular: S1/S2 audible, bradycardic. Pulses 2+ peripherally.
Respiratory: Increased work of breathing. Crackles auscultated halfway up posterior lung fields bilaterally.
Extremities: 3+ pitting edema bilateral lower extremities, extending to mid-shin.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
Chest X-ray (PA and Lateral) - Yesterday, 17:30
Findings: The cardiomediastinal silhouette is enlarged. There are prominent interstitial markings and Kerley B lines, consistent with interstitial edema. Small bilateral pleural effusions are noted. The pulmonary vasculature is congested.
Impression: Findings consistent with moderate cardiomegaly and acute pulmonary edema, indicative of congestive heart failure exacerbation.

rationale.correct: The nurse must synthesize multiple data points to ensure patient safety before administering medications. The client's bradycardia (HR 58), hypokalemia (K+ 3.2 mEq/L), and elevated creatinine (1.8 mg/dL) are critical findings. The correct actions are to place the client on a cardiac monitor due to the risk of arrhythmia, notify the provider of these critical changes, and withhold the digoxin due to the heart rate being below the standard safety threshold of 60 bpm. These actions prioritize immediate safety and collaborative communication.
rationale.incorrect: Administering the furosemide is unsafe as it will further deplete potassium, increasing the risk of digoxin toxicity and arrhythmias, and could worsen renal function given the elevated creatinine. Withholding the scheduled lisinopril is a reasonable consideration due to the low-normal blood pressure, but it is not the most immediate priority compared to the risks associated with the STAT medications and the client's unstable condition.

answerBreakdown.content: Incorrect. While the client's blood pressure is 100/60 mmHg, which is a consideration for an ACE inhibitor, the immediate life-threatening risks are related to the STAT medications, bradycardia, and electrolyte imbalance. This action is a lower priority.

Correct. The combination of hypokalemia, bradycardia, and the impending administration of IV furosemide (which wastes potassium) and digoxin places the client at high risk for life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias. Continuous cardiac monitoring is an essential safety intervention.

Incorrect. Administering a loop diuretic to a client who is already hypokalemic (K+ 3.2 mEq/L) is contraindicated without potassium replacement. It would exacerbate the hypokalemia and increase the risk of digoxin-induced arrhythmias. The elevated creatinine also warrants caution.

Correct. This is a critical action. The provider must be made aware of the constellation of findings (bradycardia, hypokalemia, elevated creatinine) that contraindicate or require modification of the STAT orders. This is a core responsibility of the nurse to ensure patient safety.

Correct. A heart rate less than 60 bpm is a standard contraindication for administering digoxin. Digoxin has negative chronotropic effects (slows the heart rate) by acting on the AV node. Administering it to a bradycardic client could cause severe bradycardia or heart block.

clinicalPearls: Always check apical pulse for a full minute before administering digoxin. Withhold the dose and notify the provider if the heart rate is <60 bpm in an adult.
Hypokalemia potentiates the effect of digoxin, increasing the risk of toxicity even with a therapeutic serum level. Furosemide is a potassium-wasting diuretic that can worsen this risk.

Acute kidney injury (indicated by rising creatinine) is a common complication in decompensated heart failure (cardiorenal syndrome). Doses of nephrotoxic or renally-cleared drugs must be adjusted or questioned.
rationale.questionTrap: This question traps test-takers who focus on a single piece of data instead of synthesizing the entire clinical picture. For example, seeing an order for furosemide for a fluid-overloaded client and acting on it without first checking the potassium and creatinine levels is a common error.
rationale.mnemonic: For high-risk medication administration, remember the 'ABC' safety check: A - Apical pulse & Allergies. B - Blood pressure & Bradycardia. C - Critical labs & Contraindications.
